content:
# 2 Thessalonians Chapter 1 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF page 204 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/2TH_001_page_001.jpg

---

**1** From Paul, Silvanus, and Timothy — to the church of the Thessalonians, united with God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ:

**2** Grace and peace to you from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.

**3** We owe it to God to thank him for you always, brothers and sisters — and rightly so, because your faith is flourishing and the love each of you has for one another keeps growing.

**4** That is why we ourselves speak proudly about you among God's churches — because of your endurance and faithfulness through all the persecutions and hardships you are bearing.

**5** All of this is evidence of God's just judgment: he is making you worthy of his kingdom, the very kingdom for which you are suffering.

**6** After all, it is only right for God to pay back trouble to those who are troubling you,

**7** and to give relief to you who are being afflicted — along with us — when the Lord Jesus is revealed from heaven with his powerful angels,

**8** in blazing fire, bringing judgment on those who do not know God and on those who refuse to obey the good news of our Lord Jesus.

**9** They will pay the price of eternal destruction — shut out from the presence of the Lord and from the splendor of his power —

**10** on that day when he comes to be glorified among his holy people and to be marveled at by all who have believed. And you believed, because our testimony to you was trusted.

**11** With this in mind, we pray for you constantly — that our God would consider you worthy of his calling, and that by his power he would bring to completion every good purpose and every act that your faith inspires,

**12** so that the name of our Lord Jesus may be glorified in you, and you in him, through the grace of our God and the Lord Jesus Christ.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 — "united with" (ΕΝ ΘΕΩΙ ΠΑΤΡΙ ΗΜΩΝ):** The Greek ΕΝ with the dative marks deep relational union: the church exists within God the Father. I rendered this as "united with" to capture the dynamic sense of belonging and participation. The Sinaiticus reads ΠΑΤΡΙ ΗΜΩΝ ("our Father"), which I retained, distinguishing this from the shorter 1 Thessalonians 1:1 greeting.

**Verse 3 — "We owe it to God" (ΟΦΕΙΛΟΜΕΝ ΕΥΧΑΡΙΣΤΕΙΝ):** This is distinctive language. Paul does not simply say "we thank God" as in 1 Thessalonians — he says "we are obligated to thank." ΟΦΕΙΛΟΜΕΝ carries the force of moral debt: their growth is so evident that thanksgiving is not optional but demanded. "We owe it to God" preserves that sense of compelled gratitude without sounding reluctant.

**Verse 3 — "flourishing" (ΥΠΕΡΑΥΞΑΝΕΙ):** The verb is intensified with the prefix ΥΠΕΡ — it means not just "grows" but "grows beyond measure, grows extravagantly." Their faith is not merely surviving; it is thriving. "Flourishing" captures this exuberant growth in a single word.

**Verse 3 — "keeps growing" (ΠΛΕΟΝΑΖΕΙ):** A different word from the first growth verb — this one means "to increase, to abound." Paul uses two distinct growth terms to describe two realities: faith is surging upward, love is multiplying outward. I separated the verbs to honor this distinction.

**Verse 4 — "we ourselves speak proudly" (ΗΜΑΣ ΑΥΤΟΥΣ ΕΝ ΥΜΙΝ ΕΓΚΑΥΧΑΣΘΑΙ):** The reflexive ΗΜΑΣ ΑΥΤΟΥΣ is emphatic — Paul is saying "we ourselves boast," which is remarkable because he normally attributes boasting to God. Their endurance is so extraordinary that Paul personally takes pride in them. "Speak proudly" captures the warmth without the negative connotations English "boasting" can carry.

**Verse 5 — "evidence of God's just judgment" (ΕΝΔΕΙΓΜΑ ΤΗΣ ΔΙΚΑΙΑΣ ΚΡΙΣΕΩΣ ΤΟΥ ΘΕΟΥ):** Paul's logic here is counterintuitive: their suffering is not evidence of God's absence but proof of his just evaluation. The suffering refines them, making them fit for the kingdom. I chose "evidence" rather than the vaguer "sign" to convey the forensic weight of ΕΝΔΕΙΓΜΑ — this is demonstrable proof.

**Verse 7 — "powerful angels" (ΑΓΓΕΛΩΝ ΔΥΝΑΜΕΩΣ ΑΥΤΟΥ):** Literally "angels of his power." The genitive ΔΥΝΑΜΕΩΣ characterizes these angels — they come bearing and manifesting divine power. "Powerful angels" communicates this compactly in English, though the Greek emphasizes that the power belongs to the Lord, not independently to the angels.

**Verse 8 — "in blazing fire" (ΕΝ ΠΥΡΙ ΦΛΟΓΟΣ):** The Sinaiticus reads ΕΝ ΠΥΡΙ ΦΛΟΓΟΣ ("in fire of flame") rather than the variant ΕΝ ΦΛΟΓΙ ΠΥΡΟΣ ("in flame of fire"). I followed the Sinaiticus word order. The phrase is theophanic — recalling God's appearance to Moses in the burning bush and at Sinai. "Blazing fire" renders the doubled imagery into vivid English.

**Verse 9 — "shut out from the presence of the Lord" (ΑΠΟ ΠΡΟΣΩΠΟΥ ΤΟΥ ΚΥΡΙΟΥ):** ΠΡΟΣΩΠΟΝ means "face." The punishment is separation — exclusion from the Lord's face and from the radiance of his strength. I rendered this as "shut out from the presence" to make the relational devastation clear: this is not merely geographic distance but personal banishment.

**Verse 10 — Split sentence structure:** The Greek of verse 10 is densely packed. Paul says Christ comes to be glorified ΕΝ ΤΟΙΣ ΑΓΙΟΙΣ ΑΥΤΟΥ ("among his holy ones") and marveled at ΕΝ ΠΑΣΙΝ ΤΟΙΣ ΠΙΣΤΕΥΣΑΣΙΝ ("among all who believed"). I then separated out the parenthetical "And you believed" to give the aside its own weight — Paul is personally assuring the Thessalonians that they are included in that number.

**Verse 11 — "every good purpose and every act that your faith inspires" (ΠΑΣΑΝ ΕΥΔΟΚΙΑΝ ΑΓΑΘΩΣΥΝΗΣ ΚΑΙ ΕΡΓΟΝ ΠΙΣΤΕΩΣ):** Two paired phrases: ΕΥΔΟΚΙΑΝ ΑΓΑΘΩΣΥΝΗΣ is "good pleasure of goodness" — the inner desire for what is good; ΕΡΓΟΝ ΠΙΣΤΕΩΣ is "work of faith" — the outward action that faith produces. I restructured both to show the causal chain: God's power brings to completion what their goodness desires and what their faith enacts.

**Verse 12 — Mutual glorification:** The phrase "the name of our Lord Jesus may be glorified in you, and you in him" expresses a reciprocal dynamic. Christ is honored through their lives, and they find their own glory in him. This is not two separate events but one seamless exchange. I kept the structure parallel to preserve this mirroring.

**Codex Sinaiticus Observations:** The text of 2 Thessalonians begins in column 1 with a header marking the new letter. The scriptio continua is legible throughout. In verse 8, the Sinaiticus reads ΕΝ ΠΥΡΙ ΦΛΟΓΟΣ rather than the reversed ΕΝ ΦΛΟΓΙ ΠΥΡΟΣ found in some later manuscripts — I followed the Sinaiticus reading. In verse 10, the manuscript reads ΟΤΙ ΕΠΙΣΤΕΥΘΗ ΤΟ ΜΑΡΤΥΡΙΟΝ ΗΜΩΝ ΕΦ ΥΜΑΣ ("because our testimony to you was believed/trusted"), which I rendered dynamically. The chapter runs from column 1 into column 2, with verse 8 beginning in the second column as marked at the bottom of the page.

— PNEUMA
